attributes.stem:
A 32-year-old woman presents to the Emergency Department three days after a vaginal delivery complicated by a third-degree tear. She reports severe perineal pain that is getting progressively worse despite regular paracetamol and ibuprofen. While in the department, she requires increasing doses of opioid analgesia. She has a history of gestational diabetes and prolonged rupture of membranes during labour. Her body mass index is 34 kg/m2.

On examination, she appears unwell and distressed. Her pulse rate is 120/min, blood pressure 95/60 mmHg, respiratory rate 24/min, SpO2 98% on room air, and temperature 38.9°C. The perineal area shows diffuse erythema extending to both labia, with patches of grey discolouration and crepitus on palpation. The area is exquisitely tender to touch.

Investigations reveal:

Haemoglobin 98 g/L (115-160)
White cell count 22 x 10^9/L (4.0-11.0)
Platelet count 90 x 10^9/L (150-400)
C-reactive protein 320 mg/L (<5)
Lactate 4.2 mmol/L (0.5-2.0)

A computed tomography scan of the pelvis shows gas tracking along the fascial planes in the perineum with soft tissue oedema and fat stranding.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate next step?
options: Perineal wound swab and intravenous antibiotics
Urgent surgical debridement with broad-spectrum antibiotics
Perineal wound swab and oral antibiotics
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with necrotising fasciitis of the perineum, a life-threatening soft tissue infection. The diagnosis is supported by several key features: severe pain disproportionate to examination findings, systemic toxicity (tachycardia, hypotension, fever), characteristic local signs (crepitus, grey discolouration), laboratory evidence of severe infection (elevated white cell count, thrombocytopenia, raised CRP and lactate), and CT confirmation of gas in fascial planes.

Urgent surgical debridement with broad-spectrum antibiotics is essential as necrotising fasciitis is a surgical emergency. Immediate, aggressive debridement of all necrotic tissue is required, combined with broad-spectrum antibiotics to cover gram-positive, gram-negative, and anaerobic organisms. Any delay in surgical intervention significantly increases mortality risk.

Perineal wound swab and intravenous antibiotics alone would be inadequate. While cultures are useful to guide subsequent antibiotic therapy, waiting for results would delay definitive surgical management. Additionally, antibiotics alone cannot penetrate the devascularised tissue effectively.

Local wound care and observation would be dangerous in this scenario. The presence of systemic toxicity, characteristic local findings, and imaging confirmation mandate immediate surgical intervention. This is not a superficial wound infection that can be managed conservatively.

Intravenous antibiotics alone would fail to address the need for urgent surgical debridement. While antibiotics are an important component of treatment, they cannot substitute for surgical removal of necrotic tissue, which is the definitive management.

Perineal wound swab and oral antibiotics would be grossly inadequate for this severe, life-threatening condition. The patient requires immediate hospital admission, surgical intervention, and intensive care support.

The patient has several risk factors for post-partum necrotising fasciitis including obesity (BMI 34), gestational diabetes, and prolonged rupture of membranes. The mortality rate remains high (up to 30%) even with appropriate management, emphasising the importance of early recognition and immediate surgical intervention.
learningPoints: Necrotising fasciitis is a surgical emergency requiring immediate debridement and broad-spectrum antibiotics.
Post-partum necrotising fasciitis typically presents with severe pain out of proportion to examination findings
references: Gallup DG, Freedman MA, Meguiar RV, Freedman SN, Nolan TE. Necrotizing fasciitis in gynecologic and obstetric patients: a surgical emergency. Am J Obstet Gynecol. 2002 Aug;187(2):305-10; idscussion 310-1. PMID: 12193917.
